skill.name: prompt-engineering-patterns
skill.description: Improve LLM prompts with reusable templates, few-shot examples, chain-of-thought patterns, system prompt guidance, and optimization workflows.
skill.summary: Build clearer, testable prompts for production LLM workflows.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk indicators, but review found the command, cryptography, reconnaissance, and sensitive-file alerts are documentation false positives. The real risks are moderate: template examples interpolate untrusted text and the sample optimizer can write result files, so publication is acceptable with a security warning.

medium_findings.title: Untrusted Prompt Template Interpolation Risk
medium_findings.description: Several examples render user-supplied values directly into prompt templates with string formatting. This is useful for prompt systems, but applications must delimit and validate untrusted input to reduce prompt injection risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The interpolation behavior is directly shown in code examples and the script. The risk depends on how a downstream application uses these templates, so it is moderate rather than high.

medium_findings.title: Local Result Export Can Overwrite Caller-Specified Files
medium_findings.description: The optimizer exposes an export function that writes JSON results to a filename supplied by the caller. This is legitimate functionality, but callers should avoid passing untrusted paths and should choose safe output locations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write is explicit and unsandboxed, but the default main path is a local report file and there is no evidence of malicious overwrite intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The many Ruby backtick execution alerts are false positives. The flagged locations are Markdown code fences and prompt examples, not Ruby code or shell command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged syntax appears inside documentation code blocks. No Ruby files, shell invocations, subprocess calls, or command strings were found at those locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Keyword Collisions
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts are false positives. The flagged lines contain ordinary prompt-engineering prose, examples, or words that include short substrings, not cryptographic APIs or hash functions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found no MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or related cryptographic implementation. The matches are context-free keyword detections in educational text.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are Benign Educational Text
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance alerts are false positives. The reviewed locations are examples, evaluation terms, or general guidance and do not execute host or network discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are Markdown or JSON examples and contain no socket, subprocess, shell, host inventory, or network scanning logic. The scanner appears to have matched isolated words.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File Alerts Are Documentation Context
low_findings.description: Certificate and key file alerts are false positives in the reviewed documentation. No private key material, credential file reads, or secret exfiltration behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are part of illustrative prompt or metrics examples. Review found no embedded secrets, certificate files, or credential access code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Untrusted Prompt Template Interpolation Risk
dangerous_patterns.description: Several examples render user-supplied values directly into prompt templates with string formatting. This is useful for prompt systems, but applications must delimit and validate untrusted input to reduce prompt injection risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The interpolation behavior is directly shown in code examples and the script. The risk depends on how a downstream application uses these templates, so it is moderate rather than high.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Result Export Can Overwrite Caller-Specified Files
dangerous_patterns.description: The optimizer exposes an export function that writes JSON results to a filename supplied by the caller. This is legitimate functionality, but callers should avoid passing untrusted paths and should choose safe output locations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file write is explicit and unsandboxed, but the default main path is a local report file and there is no evidence of malicious overwrite intent.

content.value_statement: LLM prompts often fail because instructions, examples, and evaluation criteria are unclear. This skill provides templates and workflows for improving prompts in Claude, Codex, Claude Code, and similar tools.
actual_capabilities: Explains few-shot example selection strategies, including semantic similarity, diversity sampling, and difficulty-based selection.
Provides reusable prompt templates for classification, extraction, generation, transformation, analysis, question answering, SQL, regex, and API documentation.
Documents chain-of-thought, self-consistency, verification, and tree-of-thought prompting patterns.
Shows how to structure system prompts with role, expertise, guidelines, constraints, and output format sections.
Includes a Python prompt optimization script that compares prompt variants with accuracy, latency, token, and success metrics.
Supplies example datasets for sentiment analysis, entity extraction, data transformation, summarization, and SQL generation.
limitations: The included Python script uses a mock LLM client and requires adaptation for real providers.
Prompt examples are educational and do not guarantee model safety or factual accuracy.
The material does not include a complete production evaluation platform or monitoring service.
Some code samples are illustrative and may need validation before use in applications.

use_cases.title: Standardize Product Prompts
use_cases.description: Create reusable prompt templates for support, classification, summarization, and structured generation workflows.

use_cases.title: Improve LLM Application Quality
use_cases.description: Test prompt variants with measurable accuracy, latency, token usage, and success rate metrics.

use_cases.title: Teach Prompt Design Patterns
use_cases.description: Use examples and reference guides to explain few-shot learning, reasoning prompts, and system prompt structure.

prompt_templates.title: Clarify a Simple Prompt
prompt_templates.prompt: Improve this prompt for clarity and consistency. Keep the same goal, define the expected output, and list any missing information: {prompt}

prompt_templates.title: Design a Few-Shot Prompt
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a few-shot prompt for {task}. Include three examples, consistent input and output labels, and one edge case. Explain why each example was selected.

prompt_templates.title: Build a System Prompt
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a system prompt for an assistant that handles {domain}. Include role, expertise, boundaries, output format, escalation rules, and safety constraints.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Optimization Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an optimization plan for this prompt: {prompt}. Define test cases, metrics, prompt variants, failure analysis steps, and rollout criteria.

output_examples.input: A vague support prompt for classifying customer messages.
output_examples.output: A clearer classification prompt with allowed labels, input boundaries, output format, and examples for billing, shipping, cancellation, and technical support.

output_examples.input: A system prompt for a data analyst assistant.
output_examples.output: A structured system prompt that defines analyst expertise, required assumptions, result format, uncertainty handling, and limits on unsupported claims.
An evaluation plan with representative test cases, scoring metrics, failure categories, and criteria for choosing the better prompt.

best_practices: Start with a direct prompt, then add examples, constraints, and reasoning only when tests show a need.
Use representative test cases and measure accuracy, consistency, latency, token use, and failure modes.
Treat prompt templates as product assets with versioning, documentation, and rollback plans.
anti_patterns: Do not add many examples without checking relevance, token cost, and output consistency.
Do not rely on chain-of-thought prompts for simple tasks that need concise answers.
Do not interpolate untrusted user text into high-privilege prompt sections without clear boundaries.

faq.question: Does this skill run external services?
faq.answer: No external service is called by the skill materials. The sample script uses a mock LLM client unless a developer adapts it.

faq.question: Can it help with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The patterns are general prompt design methods that apply across Claude, Codex, Claude Code, and other LLM tools.

faq.question: Does it include ready-made prompt templates?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes templates for classification, extraction, generation, analysis, question answering, SQL, regex, and documentation tasks.

faq.question: Does it include evaluation guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers baseline testing, A/B testing, metrics, failure analysis, and prompt versioning workflows.

faq.question: Can I use the Python script as-is in production?
faq.answer: No. The script is a demonstration and should be reviewed, tested, and adapted before production use.

faq.question: What is the main security caution?
faq.answer: Applications should not place untrusted user input into privileged prompt sections without delimiters, validation, and injection-resistant design.
